recommendation: For an in-process, single-package app: use FFI + callback for the hot streaming path, SQLite (WAL) for durable history, and treat HTTP as the comparison harness (gRPC is kept as reference but excluded from the build) rather than the production transport.

patterns.name: FFI + Callback
patterns.summary: The native UI calls the NativeAOT C exports directly in memory and passes a function pointer; .NET invokes it once per token. No sockets, no serialization, no disk.
patterns.bestFor: The production hot path: lowest-latency token streaming in a single package.
features: Zero IPC — direct in-memory calls, no serialization, no loopback, no disk
Lowest latency and highest throughput of all the patterns
Fewest moving parts at runtime: no server to bind, start, or stop
Bounded-channel backpressure maps cleanly onto the synchronous callback
No extra OS permissions; nothing ever leaves the process
limitations: Most complex to build: manual UTF-8 marshalling, GC lifetimes, function-pointer ABI
Callback fires on a .NET background thread — hopping to the UI thread is the caller's job
Android needs a C/JNI shim (.NET can't call Kotlin directly) with a cached JavaVM* + AttachCurrentThread
Shared address space: a managed crash takes down the host process
No process isolation or sandbox boundary between UI and engine

patterns.name: Framed Protobuf
patterns.summary: The library starts a raw-socket server on a dynamic 127.0.0.1 port that speaks length-prefixed Google.Protobuf frames ([u32 LE length][Envelope]); the UI sends typed request Envelopes and reads typed responses — structured binary RPC with no ASP.NET or gRPC runtime. An opt-in ML-KEM-768 / ML-DSA-65 handshake then AES-256-GCM-encrypts every frame.
patterns.bestFor: A strongly-typed binary boundary the gRPC dead-end wanted to be — schema-driven, AOT-clean, shippable on mobile; and the place to demonstrate a post-quantum secure channel.
features: Strongly-typed protobuf contract (the schema gRPC uses) with none of the Kestrel/ASP.NET stack — ships under NativeAOT
Compact binary framing instead of HTTP/1.1 text + JSON: smaller on the wire than the HTTP transport
Server-streaming maps naturally onto the token stream via a sequence of chunk frames
Opt-in post-quantum channel: ML-KEM-768 key exchange + ML-DSA-65 handshake signature, AES-256-GCM per frame
Typed ErrorFrame payloads — failures surface as data, never a silent socket close
limitations: Still a socket to talk to yourself in-process: TCP + framing + protobuf (de)serialization overhead vs FFI
Dynamic port handshake: the UI must read back the OS-assigned port
Backgrounding kills the listener (same as HTTP) — the socket must be restarted on foreground
PQ mode is fixed at server start (per-boot keys); switching plaintext<->PQ restarts the server
The handshake authenticates transcript integrity, not server identity (trust-on-first-use) — loopback only

patterns.name: HTTP Loopback
patterns.summary: The library starts a minimal raw-socket HTTP/1.1 server (no ASP.NET) on a dynamic 127.0.0.1 port; the UI reads tokens back as Server-Sent Events.
patterns.bestFor: Fast prototyping and debuggability; a familiar boundary anyone can curl.
features: Familiar REST/SSE model; debuggable with curl or a proxy
Language-agnostic boundary using stock URLSession / OkHttp
Server-Sent Events map naturally onto a token stream
Loopback (127.0.0.1) does NOT trigger the iOS Local Network permission prompt
Hand-rolled raw-socket server — no ASP.NET runtime pack needed, small binary
limitations: TCP + HTTP framing + JSON overhead just to talk to yourself in-process
Dynamic port handshake: the UI must read back the OS-assigned port
iOS suspends the listener on backgrounding — the socket dies and must be restarted on foreground
You hand-roll HTTP/1.1 parsing and SSE framing (no ASP.NET on mobile) — minimal but unvalidated
One-directional SSE; no backpressure beyond the TCP window

patterns.name: gRPC over UDS
patterns.transport: Kestrel gRPC on a Unix Domain Socket (EXCLUDED from build)
patterns.summary: Reference design only — excluded from the build because ASP.NET Core gRPC has no NativeAOT mobile runtime pack. Would host a gRPC server bound to a .sock in the app sandbox, with the UI as a server-streaming client.
patterns.bestFor: A strongly-typed, schema-driven boundary without exposing a TCP port (not shippable on mobile NativeAOT today).
features: Strongly-typed protobuf contract; server-streaming fits token streams perfectly
Unix Domain Socket stays inside the sandbox: no TCP port, no Local Network prompt
Efficient binary framing over HTTP/2; mature clients (grpc-swift, grpc-java)
Clear schema-evolution and versioning story
limitations: No NativeAOT mobile runtime pack — cannot ship on iOS/Android (the reason it is excluded)
Serializes protobuf to talk to yourself across a socket in the same process
Pulls the full Kestrel + ASP.NET + gRPC stack into the lib — largest binary, most AOT-trim care
gRPC client dependencies (grpc-swift/NIO, grpc-java) add app size
UDS lifecycle: must place the socket in the sandbox and clean up stale files

patterns.name: SQLite WAL Broker
patterns.transport: Shared embedded SQLite (WAL) as a message broker
patterns.summary: The UI writes a request row; a .NET background loop observes the table, runs inference, and writes streaming tokens back to a response table the UI polls.
patterns.bestFor: Durable, decoupled request/response history and offline job queues.
features: Durable: requests and tokens survive an app kill; full transcript persists for free
Naturally reactive and decoupled; tolerates producer/consumer restarts
WAL mode lets the UI read while the broker writes concurrently
Trivial to inspect and debug — it's just a .db file; fully language-agnostic
Excellent as a job queue or offline outbox
limitations: Polling latency: tokens appear at the poll interval, not the instant they're produced
Per-token row INSERT is write amplification (WAL growth, disk churn) — wrong for hot streaming
No push; the reader must poll, costing battery and wakeups
You own the ordering, status, retention, and vacuum logic
Highest end-to-end latency of the patterns
